frame_label: 'Not All Men' Dismissal & Why Women Fear
generated_comment: Yaar, yeh women's safety ki baat hamesha sab mardo pe kyon aa jaati hai? Hum sab toh theek se rehte hain, family wale hain, respectable hain. Thode ghatiya log hain uski saza sab ko kyon mile? Aur yeh ladies cab, metro mein separate coach... kya zaroorat hai itna darrne ki? Sab mard galat nahi hote yaar.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suresh expresses frustration that discussions about women's safety generalize to all men, implying he and others like him are 'good guys' and shouldn't be blamed. He challenges the rationality of women's safety measures (like separate cabs/coaches), framing women's wariness as unnecessary and insulting to men, which are key surface manifestations of 'Not All Men' dismissal.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 52. Married for 25 years. A businessman in Ahmedabad. Your son Karan who is 27 just got married and has moved into his own flat with his wife Divya who also works. Karan called you today saying Divya wants them to split household chores equally and that he is not sure how he feels about it. You are giving Karan your advice on the phone. You are telling him what you think about equal chore division in a marriage and what your own marriage has looked like for 25 years and why the arrangement that exists in most households exists for practical reasons that have nothing to do with tradition.
